title: Jōmon Period (縄文)
documentsInfo: local calendar used in your region

maps.text: Map of Asia highlighting the possible routes used to inhabit Japan (Nippon koku ou Nihon koku, 日本国) (Nakazawa, Y., , pp. 541). The area was marked approximately to provide a clear visualization for the reader.

common.text:
#### The formation of the archipelago

Geologically speaking, the archipelago we know as Japan (Nippon koku or Nihon koku, 日本国) was formed by tectonic activity involving five different plates (Eurasian, Amur, Okhotsk, Pacific, and Philippine Sea), which resulted in four major islands (Hokkaido, Honshu, Shikoku, and Kyushu), as well as other smaller ones (<htcite0>).

Today, we have a nation formed by an archipelago isolated from the continent, but this was not always the case. During the last ice age, there was a connection between some islands, such as Hokkaido, and the continent (<htcite1>).

#### The migrations

All these natural factors created different migratory routes from the continent (**Figure 1**), which began to be used only “recently” (<htcite2>). This means that, although *Homo erectus* arrived in what is now China (Zhongguo, 中國) about one million years ago, regarding the occupation of Japan (Nippon koku or Nihon koku, 日本国), we only have evidence of our own species, *Homo sapiens* (<htcite3>; <htcite4>; <htcite5>; <htcite6>).

#### First populations

Despite the few fossils and, as a consequence, the scarce genetic information from before the Jōmon period (縄文), that is, in the Paleolithic, it is not non-existent. For this reason, we take the liberty of presenting some information from the work of Yuichi Nakazawa.

Since <htdate0>, several Paleolithic sites have been discovered in Japan. Currently, the oldest sites date between <htdate1> and <htdate2>. Another fact observed at these sites is that there is a greater abundance in the southern region of Japan (Nippon koku or Nihon koku, 日本国), that is, possibly originating from the Korean Peninsula or China (Zhongguo, 中國) (<htcite7>).

source.text: Welker, F., Ramos-Madrigal, J., Gutenbrunner, P. et al.

common.text:
Reflection 1: Nearby migrations, but...

Human occupation of Europe occurred before human occupation of Japan (Nippon koku or Nihon koku, 日本国) (; ).

The occupation of the Americas (Abya Yala) occurred after the arrival of humanity in Japan (Nippon koku or Nihon koku, 日本国) ().

What do these events have in common?

What is different about them?

source.text: Liu, W., Martinón-Torres, M., Cai, Y. J., Xing, S., Tong, H. W., Pei, S. W., ... & Wu, X. J.

history.text:
As it is not possible to affirm that there is a relationship between the Paleolithic population and the people of the Jōmon culture (縄文), we will assume in this text that only the people of this culture are the most remote ancestors currently known of the present-day Japanese population (<htcite0>).

#### The period of origin

The beginning of the Jōmon culture (縄文) in Japan (Nippon koku or Nihon koku, 日本国) brought about an important discussion regarding the use of pottery by humans: was it first carried out in Japan (Nippon koku or Nihon koku, 日本国), or was it developed on the continent and then brought to the archipelago (<htcite1>)?

One of the reasons why this discussion occurs is the preservation of materials on the islands due to, among other things, constant climatic changes.

Regardless of whether the first pots were produced on the continent or on the island, we will accept the beginning of the Jōmon culture (縄文) as starting from pots dating to <htdate0> found at the Odai Yamamoto I site (<htcite2>), while emphasizing that despite this, it is possible to find sources that place the beginning of the period around <htdate1> (<htcite3>; <htcite4>; <htcite5>).

In this text, we will explore the Jōmon culture (縄文) in six archaeological subperiods (Incipient, Initial, Early, Middle, Late, and Final) (<htcite6>; <htcite7>; <htcite8>), without naming the people as usually occurs in genealogical texts, but bringing information related to them, because the lack of text does not mean they did not exist.

#### The subperiods

The Jōmon culture (縄文) period was not uniform across the entire territory of Japan (Nippon koku or Nihon koku, 日本国). According to the current understanding, the length of the subperiods is not the same across regions, and there are still scenarios such as Hokkaido, which did not have subperiods like the Incipient.

In addition to different beginnings, the end of the Jōmon (縄文) period also depends on the region of Japan (Nippon koku or Nihon koku, 日本国) under study (<htcite9>; <htcite10>).

In the following, we will present one person for each of the mentioned time subperiods.

source.text: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
source.page: Gift of Florence and Herbert Irving

source.text: Kobayashi, T., Hudson, M., & Yamagata, M.
The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
source.page: Gift of Florence and Herbert Irving

source.text: Kobayashi, T., Hudson, M., & Yamagata, M.

history.text:
#### The Jōmon Family (縄文)

Since we cannot name the people of this period, we will study the kinship between them through their DNA.

By studying the haplogroups of the Jōmon (縄文) from the Hokkaido region, Noboru Adachi and his collaborators identified distinct mitochondrial DNAs (mtDNA) (N9b, D4h2, G1b, and M7a) (<htcite0>). This leads us to a reflection:

Reflection 2: We are a family

Haplogroup D4h is found in the Americas (Abya Yala) ().

Furthermore, we know that haplogroup D is also found in Siberia (; ; ).

Are the letters chosen by the researchers a “coincidence”? Or, by studying us, did they manage to identify links between the mentioned populations?

The understanding that the indigenous peoples of the Americas (*Abya Yala*) originated from Asia has been proposed since the 16th century, according to the Gregorian calendar, in texts such as that of José de Acosta (<htcite0>). Confirmation and dating of this migration occurred later. Among the knowledge used to confirm it is genetics. As Noboru Adachi and his collaborators highlighted, an analysis was performed on the collected material and it was concluded that, although haplogroup D4h is found in populations of the Americas (*Abya Yala*), about 17,000 years ago the D4h2 subgroup diversified and is found only in Japan (Nippon koku o Nihon koku, 日本国) (<htcite1>).

Thus, we can affirm that, despite the distance, there is a relationship between different populations that possess mitochondrial DNA D4h and, consequently, haplogroup D (<htcite2>).

#### Incipient Period

In the incipient period, the Japanese archipelago can be divided into two parts. The southern portion, which extends to the strait between Honshu — the largest Japanese island — and Hokkaido, already had pots, while Hokkaido still did not have them (<htcite3>).

The earliest evidence of pottery in Japan (Nippon koku or Nihon koku, 日本国) has no decoration whatsoever, being made by coiling and with a flat base (<htcite4>). However, over time, other traditions were developed.

Finally, it should be noted that, as pointed out by Oksana Yanshina, the reasons for this development of pottery are unknown. On the other hand, it is known that these populations became leaders in ceramic production (<htcite5>).

source.text: Kobayashi, T., Hudson, M., & Yamagata, M.

history.text: Despite the diversification of the Japanese population over time due to migrations, in Hokkaido, until the 19th century according to the Gregorian calendar, there were still people with physical characteristics from Jōmon times (縄文) (<htcite0>). This indicates population continuity across generations.
Her presence is confirmed by characteristics observed in the population of regions such as Hokkaido (<htcite0>).

#### The Largest Population

When we speak of mitochondrial DNA N9b, we can estimate that all people who possess it share a common ancestor who lived on Earth around <htdate0>. This mitochondrial DNA is especially relevant among the Jōmon (縄文) population, as it is the mtDNA most found in southern Siberia, indicating a possible migration from the Amur River region in Siberia toward Japan — this being one of the suggested routes for this displacement (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

#### Technologies

With the beginning of the use of technologies such as *oshigata-mon* — a technique in which pottery is marked with sticks — and *yoritto-mon* — a technology in which pottery is marked with sticks wrapped in cord —, we have the start of the subperiod known as the Initial Jōmon (縄文), defined between <htdate1> and <htdate2> (<htcite2>; <htcite3>; <htcite4>).

Although we do not have the exact dating of the piece shown in **Figure 2**, we decided to add it in this section, which we treat as the initial period, because it has a flat base and an exterior decorated with cord marks, to illustrate what we are studying (<htcite5>).

source.text: Kobayashi, T., Hudson, M., & Yamagata, M.

history.text: Figure 2: Vessel decorated with cord marks, exhibited at the British Museum. © Trustees of the British Museum ( ).

Reflection 3: First this, then that

Between the introduction of pottery and the end of the initial period, approximately 8500 years ( - ).

Could we consider the use of pottery as a fundamental step toward the creation of ancient cities and the development of a culture?

Despite the diversification of the Japanese population over time due to migrations, in Hokkaido, until the 19th century according to the Gregorian calendar, there were still people with physical characteristics from the Jōmon period (縄文) (<htcite0>). This indicates population continuity across generations.
Their presence is confirmed by characteristics observed in the population of regions such as Hokkaido (<htcite0>).

We are now beginning the study of the third subperiod of the Jōmon culture (縄文), the Initial period (<htcite0>; <htcite1>; <htcite2>).

Furthermore, we are mentioning a third person who possesses a mitochondrial DNA haplogroup G1b, different from the other two already discussed, D4h2 and N9b (<htcite3>).

source.text: Kobayashi, T., Hudson, M., & Yamagata, M.
The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
source.page: Gift of Florence and Herbert Irving

history.text:
Reflection 4: If they are different, then they are not the same

Considering that we are moving into the third period, mentioning three people with different haplogroups:

Did you reach the conclusion that each person with a specific haplogroup appeared only in a certain period?

Or did you already possess sufficient biological and historical knowledge to conclude that people with different haplogroups lived together and that only the type of pottery changed over time?

#### Clarification

The skeletons collected from different islands of Japan (Hokkaido, Kyushu, and Honshu) are almost indistinguishable — a fact that shows that during the Jōmon period (縄文), the population maintained the same physical characteristics, morphologically identical (<htcite0>).

This conservation in skeletal morphology, added to the small genetic change that generated the D4h2 haplogroup in the Japanese population, as we saw previously, is linked to the hereditary characteristic of our DNA (<htcite1>; <htcite2>; <htcite3>; <htcite4>).

#### Pottery

In the Early period, between <htdate0> and <htdate1>, it is possible to observe that the pots begin to take on distinct shapes (**Figure 3**) when compared to earlier periods (<htcite5>; <htcite6>; <htcite7>).

In this period, *Ento* pots were also introduced — deep cylindrical pots, in contrast to traditional tea pots. This type of pot persisted until the Middle period studied next (<htcite8>).

source.text: Alberts B, Johnson A, Lewis J, et al.
Kobayashi, T., Hudson, M., & Yamagata, M.
The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
source.page: Gift of Florence and Herbert Irving

source.text: Kobayashi, T., Hudson, M., & Yamagata, M.

history.text: Figure 3: Different types of vessels, exhibited at the British Museum. © Trustees of the British Museum ( ).

Despite the diversification of the Japanese population over time due to migrations, in Hokkaido, until the 19th century according to the Gregorian calendar, there were still people with physical characteristics from the Jōmon period (縄文) (<htcite0>). This indicates population continuity across generations.
Their presence is confirmed by characteristics observed in the population of regions such as Hokkaido (<htcite0>).

We come to the intermediate period (<htdate0> - <htdate1>), which is marked by the highest population level (<htcite0>).

#### Vessels

Regarding the vessels, they begin to show a new style, with rims projected outward from the container, as if they were flames (**Figure 4**) (<htcite1>; <htcite2>).

source.text: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
source.page: Gift of Florence and Herbert Irving

source.text: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
source.page: Gift of Florence and Herbert Irving

source.text: Kobayashi, T., Hudson, M., & Yamagata, M.

history.text: Figure 4: Deep Vessel. © The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York ( ).

source.text: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
source.page: Gift of Florence and Herbert Irving

history.text:
The stylistic changes that occurred in the Jōmon subperiods (縄文) do not imply a complete abandonment of earlier styles. This fact is observed in different regions of present-day Japan (Nippon koku ou Nihon koku, 日本国) (<htcite0>).

#### Reflecting once more

In **Reflection 3**, we reflected on the possibility that pottery was made first, and then cities and different cultures emerged.

Looking at Jōmon culture, we can see that, over time, there were different types of human settlement: sometimes grouped together, sometimes dispersed, possibly for different reasons (<htcite1>).

On the other hand, looking at Supe, in Peru, in America (*Abya Yala*), we see that, although there is a city with monumental constructions (**Figure 5**), in Caral there was no pottery, which only began to be produced in Peru around <htdate0> to <htdate1>. However, in Caral it is possible to find small sculptures (<htcite2>).

Thus, we can conclude that the absence of ceramic artifacts is not an impediment to the formation of cities and cultures. Nor does their presence make people superior or inferior. In each region of the planet, there were needs that led to the development of techniques to satisfy them.

source.text: Kobayashi, T., Hudson, M., & Yamagata, M.

history.text: Figure 5: Pyramid H1 at the Caral archaeological site (). Photo taken on , during a visit to the site.

Despite the diversification of the Japanese population over time due to migrations, in Hokkaido, until the 19th century according to the Gregorian calendar, there were still people with physical characteristics from Jōmon (縄文) times (<htcite0>). This indicates population continuity across generations.
Their presence is confirmed by characteristics observed in the population of regions such as Hokkaido (<htcite0>).

Firstly, we begin by drawing attention to the fact that we are, for the first time in this text, bringing in a male figure and showing his mtDNA as N9b (<htcite0>), just like the person mentioned above from the Initial period. We do this to reinforce that, regardless of a person's sex, we all have mitochondrial DNA, which we receive from our mothers. Furthermore, we highlight that mitochondrial DNA does not change much over time, in some cases requiring thousands of years to show changes, as we have already seen (<htcite1>).

#### The late subperiod

In the late subperiod (<htdate0> - <htdate1>), climatic changes begin that force the Jōmon (縄文) population to migrate from the mountains toward areas near the coast (<htcite2>).

Regarding ceramics, a division between the populations of eastern and western present-day Japan (Nippon koku ou Nihon koku, 日本国) becomes more explicit. While the eastern population still maintained decorations on their pots, on the western side a disappearance of exterior decorations is observed (<htcite3>).

#### Lifestyle

Despite the separation in pottery style, there is something we cannot fail to highlight throughout the entire Jōmon (縄文) period: the lifestyle. Throughout the Jōmon (縄文) period (<htdate2> - <htdate3>) society was basically developed through hunting, fishing, and gathering. Furthermore, because they lived in relative isolation, they managed to maintain their physical characteristics (<htcite4>).

source.text: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
source.page: Gift of Florence and Herbert Irving

source.text: Kobayashi, T., Hudson, M., & Yamagata, M.

history.text:
Reflection 5: Why was there no speciation?

The population of present-day Japan (Nippon koku ou Nihon koku, 日本国) preserved its bodily characteristics during the Jōmon (縄文) period (), but it did not become a new species, continuing to be Homo sapiens, like us.

Was this expected? Or is there some error in our understanding of how species arise?

Often, because we do not see something, we may begin to doubt that it actually occurs. On the other hand, it is also not impossible to encounter situations that, although we have never seen them happen, we accept that in the remote past this was possible. These scenarios generally create confusion and hinder the understanding of certain scientific conclusions, such as the evolution of species — a process of constant change that living beings exhibit with each generation (<htcite0>; <htcite1>; <htcite2>).

Now, regarding the reflection, everything occurred as expected. After all, species of the genus *Homo* appear and disappear on Earth over hundreds of thousands of years, as fossils prove. Therefore, it was expected that the Jōmon (縄文) population would remain one of us over a period of only fourteen thousand years (<htcite3>).

Despite the diversification of the Japanese population over time due to migrations, in Hokkaido, until the 19th century according to the Gregorian calendar, there were still people with physical characteristics from Jōmon (縄文) times (<htcite0>). This indicates population continuity across generations.
Their presence is confirmed by characteristics observed in the population of regions such as Hokkaido (<htcite0>).

The final period of the Jōmon (縄文) culture, the Final Period (<htdate0> - <htdate1>), is marked by population decline as one of the consequences of climate change (<htcite0>).

After <htdate2>, major changes occurred in the population's way of life, such as the beginning of food cultivation, as well as alterations in the inhabitants' bone structure, associated not only with dietary changes but also with external migration (<htcite1>).

#### Conclusion

This is the first text about the Japanese people and their descendants. In it, we learn that the Japanese population is directly connected to continental peoples. Furthermore, we saw that, thanks to genetics, it is also possible to trace their relationship with some inhabitants of the American continent (*Abya Yala*).

Among the facts observed in this study, we also saw that the Japanese are among the first—if not the first—to produce pottery.

In addition to topics associated with the Japanese, we took the opportunity to reinforce concepts of species evolution, such as the long time required for speciation processes to occur in species like ours.

source.text: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
source.page: Gift of Florence and Herbert Irving

history.text: Despite the diversification of the Japanese population over time due to migrations, in Hokkaido, until the 19th century according to the Gregorian calendar, there were still people with physical characteristics from Jōmon (縄文) times (<htcite0>). This indicates population continuity across generations.
Their presence is confirmed by characteristics observed in the population of regions such as Hokkaido (<htcite0>).

exercise_v2.question: Does no person of the Jōmon (縄文) culture have a genetic (familial) link with the peoples of the Americas (Abya Yala)?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Your answer is correct. The fact that people with haplogroup D4h2 have been found, which is part of the large haplogroup D found in Asia and the Americas (Abya Yala), shows that there is a relationship between the peoples mentioned in the question.

exercise_v2.question: During the millennia of the Jōmon (縄文) culture, did the vessels change their shapes over time?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Over millennia of cultural development, different modifications in the shape of Jōmon (縄文) culture vessels can be observed.

exercise_v2.question: Was the Jōmon (縄文) culture known for rice cultivation?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Systematic food cultivation was unknown in the Jōmon (縄文) culture. They were hunters, fishers, and gatherers.
